w:r.w:t: Kalasalingam Academy of Research and Education, T
Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

This project presents an end-to-end AI-powered Waste Management System designed to streamline urban sanitation by connecting citizens directly with municipal authorities. The ecosystem consists of a user-facing mobile application that utilizes Google’s Gemini AI to automatically detect waste types, estimate quantities, and provide disposal instructions from captured images, while seamlessly logging geolocation data to the cloud. Complementing this is a dedicated administrative dashboard for authorities that receives real-time alerts via Firebase Cloud Messaging, allowing officials to monitor, verify, and resolve reported issues efficiently. Built with a robust technology stack featuring Flutter, FastAPI, and Cloud Firestore, the system fosters a proactive approach to cleanliness by transforming community reporting into actionable data for rapid response.

Managing waste has become a serious challenge in today’s cities due to rapid population growth, urbanization, and changing lifestyles. Every day, a large amount of waste is generated, and when it is not handled properly, it leads to problems such as land and water pollution, bad odors, health risks, and the spread of diseases. One of the main reasons for this situation is poor waste segregation at the source and delayed action from municipal authorities.

In most cities, waste collection still depends on manual inspection and fixed schedules. These traditional methods do not provide real-time information about waste accumulation, which often results in garbage being left unattended for long periods. This creates unhygienic surroundings and affects the quality of life of people. To overcome these limitations, there is a strong need for smart, efficient, and technology-based waste management solutions.

This project proposes a Smart Waste Segregation System using a Mobile Application to make waste identification and management easier and faster. The system uses a smartphone camera along with image processing and machine learning techniques to detect and classify waste. Users can simply take a photo of the waste through the mobile application, and the system automatically identifies the type of waste, such as biodegradable, non-biodegradable, recyclable, or hazardous.
The mobile application is designed with two separate login modules to improve coordination and responsibility.

The User Login allows citizens to capture and upload images of waste found in public areas. After the image is uploaded, the application classifies the waste and sends the details along with the location to the municipal authorities. This encourages public participation and creates awareness about proper waste segregation.

The Municipality Authority Login enables officials to receive waste reports in real time. Based on these reports, cleaning staff can be assigned to the required locations. Once the waste is cleared, the authority updates the status in the application as cleaned or pending, ensuring transparency and better monitoring.

Overall, the proposed system improves communication between citizens and municipal bodies, reduces response time, and increases the efficiency of waste collection and segregation. By using image-based waste detection, the system reduces human effort and errors, supports effective recycling, and contributes to a cleaner, healthier, and smarter urban environment in line with Smart City initiatives.
The system is implemented using a

comprising a Client Application (User), a Processing Server (Backend), and an Administrative Dashboard (Authorities). The workflow follows a linear pipeline of data acquisition, intelligent processing, and actionable distribution.

The process begins in the User Application (Flutter), where the user captures an image of the waste via the device camera. This image is transmitted to the FastAPI backend through a secure REST endpoint (/detect). The backend acts as a gateway to Google’s Gemini 2.5 Flash model. We utilize prompt engineering to instruct the model to analyze the image specifically for waste management criteria, returning a structured JSON response containing the waste type, estimated quantity, and recommended disposal methods.
Upon user confirmation of the AI-generated analysis, the data is submitted to the

endpoint. To ensure system performance and minimize storage costs, the backend implements an image compression algorithm using the Pillow library (PIL), resizing images to a maximum of 1024px and applying Lanczos resampling with dynamic quality adjustment to keep file sizes under 700KB. The optimized image is encooded to Base64, and the complete record (metadata + image) is stored in Google Cloud Firestore.

The Authorities Application receives the notification and retrieves the full incident details from Firestore. The dashboard renders these reports in real-time, displaying the location, waste severity, and imagery, enabling officials to triage tasks and deploy cleanup crews efficiently.

The platform's user interface was created using Flutter and Dart, providing a seamless and responsive mobile experience for both citizens reporting waste and authorities managing disposal tasks. Python (FastAPI) is used to implement the robust backend, which manages API request processing, image optimization, and the coordination of data flow. All incident reports, captured imagery, and status updates are organized in a Cloud Firestore database, serving as a centralized and real-time source of truth for the application.

To improve functioning and intelligence, the system incorporates advanced external services. The Google Gemini AI module (Flash model) is integrated to perform deep image analysis, automatically identifying waste types, estimating quantities, and suggesting disposal methods. Location services are utilized to tag precise GPS coordinates to every report, while Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) is used to guarantee prompt coordination through instant push notifications, alerting authorities immediately when new waste is detected.
The AI Food Donation Platform's functional performance, dependability, and real-time responsiveness are the main objectives of the experimental design.
Determining how well the system manages concurrent donation submissions, NGO matching, status updates, and AI recommendation requests under typical usage settings is the primary goal.
Donors posted food donations, NGOs verified pickups, and the system handled location recognition and alerts in a number of test scenarios that mimicked real-world circumstances.
User interaction flow, database consistency, server response time, and donor-NGO proximity matching accuracy were all assessed.
he test guarantees that the platform functions effectively in practical settings and facilitates

Without the requirement for specialist hardware, the platform was created and tested in a typical computer environment. SQLite was the main database used to store user profiles and donation details, and Django was used to install the backend server locally. Participants

in the test, who represented NGOs and donors, used a web browser and a regular Wi-Fi connection to access the platform. To guarantee consistent display and speed, the interface was tested in a variety of browsers. This configuration verifies that high-performance infrastructure is not necessary for the system to be implemented on widely accessible computing resources.

The server's response time for status updates and donation submissions stayed between one and two seconds, demonstrating effective backend request processing. The location-based matching algorithm's accuracy was about 92%, with insufficient NGO location data being the main cause of mismatches.
Testing Environment and Result Data Collection

The results of the smart waste segregation application were obtained through real-time testing using a mobile phone. The application was tested by capturing images of commonly found waste items such as paper, plastic bottles, plastic covers, and metal cans. These images were taken in different environments including indoor areas, roadsides, and public places to reflect real-world conditions.

Users logged into the application using the user login module and captured images using the mobile camera. Once the image was captured, it was processed by the application and classified into one of the three categories: paper, plastic, or metal. Each successful classification was stored in the system database along with the time and waste type.

The collected data included the total number of waste images uploaded, the classified waste type, and the response from the municipality authority. All this data was exported to MS Excel for analysis. This structured approach helped in organizing the results clearly and allowed easy plotting of graphs. The testing environment and data collection process ensured that the results truly represent how the application would perform in real-world usage.

The waste classification results show that the application performs well in identifying paper, plastic, and metal waste using the mobile camera. Plastic waste was classified most accurately because of its distinct shape and texture. Items such as plastic bottles and wrappers were easily identified by the system.

Paper waste classification also showed good results, especially for newspapers and cardboard. However, when paper was crumpled or mixed with other materials, the classification accuracy slightly reduced. Metal waste classification performed consistently, although reflective surfaces sometimes caused minor misclassification. Despite this, the system was able to correctly identify most metal objects such as cans and containers.

using bar graphs and line graphs. These graphs clearly show the number of waste items correctly identified under each category. Overall, the results confirm that the application can reliably classify waste types with minimal error. This automatic classification reduces the need for manual waste segregation and helps in promoting cleaner waste management practices.
Based on freshness and type indicators, the AI assistant offered recommendations for food safety and storage.
Recommendations, according to users, were significant and consistent with realistic handling techniques.
recommending when food should be securely disposed of, reused, or conserved, the AI module directly reduced waste.
Mobile app accessibility and volunteer-based pickup routing to facilitate broader adoption were identified as issues for future improvement.
The suggested platform's performance and operational results are consistent with recent research on community-based sustainability networks and AI-assisted food redistribution.

Donor-recipient connectivity is the main emphasis of such systems in the literature currently in publication; nonetheless, the majority lack automated decision support and real-time coordination methods. In contrast, the current method enhances responsiveness and dependability by combining AI-supported food handling guidance with geolocation-based NGO matching.

This platform's integration of live coordination, automated matching, and AI-driven food preservation advice into a unified operational workflow is a major innovation. By guaranteeing prompt pickup and well-informed handling choices, the system actively minimizes food waste rather of merely serving as a reporting or listing interface. Accountability is improved and uncertainty between donors and NGOs is decreased when the donation status flow is transparent. Furthermore, the platform's browser-based interface and low hardware dependency allow for wide accessibility, which makes it appropriate for scaled deployment in a variety of community settings. The AI module's capacity to offer practical storage and reuse suggestions sets the system apart from traditional applications for redistributing excess food.

Although the system works well in moderate and controlled real-world testing, there are still a number of issues. The completeness and accuracy of stored location and availability data determine how well NGO matching works.

Reliability would be increased by including automated verification and dynamic NGO capacity reporting. Extending the system to mobile applications would improve accessibility for field workers and low-resource users, as the current deployment primarily uses web platforms. Predictive demand estimation, volunteer-based pickup routing, and the incorporation of bigger food quality information to boost the customization of AI recommendations are possible future improvements. Large-scale longitudinal research and wider field deployment are needed to confirm system resilience and community impact.
By facilitating real-time coordination between donors and NGOs through automated location-based matching and live status tracking, the proposed AI Food Donation

Platform tackles the issue of excess food waste. The Gemini AI module's integration reduces waste by offering context-aware advice for food preservation and appropriate eating. The platform functions dependably with minimal response latency, correct NGO assignment, and high user approval, according to experimental evaluation, suggesting that it is appropriate for real-world deployment. In order to facilitate broader operational scalability, future work will concentrate on creating a mobile application to increase accessibility, integrating dynamic NGO capacity reports, and enabling volunteer-assisted pickup routing.

may be further enhanced by adding richer datasets to the AI module. All things considered, the platform offers a scalable and efficient solution for community-based food redistribution and closely corresponds with global sustainability goals, especially SDG2 and SDG12
